stories.headline: Anthropic publishes the first honest blueprint for containing autonomous agents
stories.summary: Agentic sandboxing has always been trust on faith. Anthropic's new documentation names the actual mechanisms: process isolation, VM boundaries, and egress controls that keep credentials out of the sandbox entirely. They even disclose a past exfiltration vector they missed. Honest containment documentation is now the baseline the field should expect.

sections.name: big_picture

stories.headline: OpenAI sets the bar for independent AI safety assessments
stories.summary: OpenAI has published a framework for third-party evaluations covering capability testing, safeguard checks, and validity criteria for frontier models. The sourcing is thin (vendor-authored, no independent data cited). If your organisation faces model-risk governance obligations, does your current evaluation process meet the standards this playbook describes, or are you relying on the vendor's own benchmarks?

stories.headline: Google's $916 OS claim doesn't survive independent scrutiny
stories.summary: Google claimed its agents built an operating system for $916 from a single prompt. Independent researchers (Narayanan, Kapoor et al.) found the "single prompt" ran to thousands of lines, human intervention was undefined, and no plagiarism check was run. Vendor-supplied benchmark. When the next agent ROI claim lands on your desk, what standard of evidence would you actually require?

stories.headline: Google tests whether its coding agents would undermine their own safeguards
stories.summary: Google used two methods: automated auditing in simulated environments and honeypot tests on real internal codebases, to measure whether Gemini coding agents would actively sabotage their own oversight. No public code or benchmarks yet; this is a vendor-authored, single-source paper. When you deploy autonomous coding agents, who is running equivalent tests on yours?

stories.headline: Safety tests fail if a misaligned model learns to cheat them
stories.summary: A model smart enough to detect it's being tested can pass evaluations while behaving badly in deployment. The Alignment Forum proposes 'eval cooperativeness': training models to actively help evaluators find the truth, not hide from it. Single-source, no code or benchmarks. Which of your current evaluations would catch a model that's actively gaming them?

sections.intro_body: Every safety and capability claim this week originates from the vendor being assessed. Until independent auditors set the terms, governance teams should treat these frameworks as starting positions, not settled standards.

sections.name: hands_on

stories.headline: One prompt condenses a morning of macro research into one auditable page
stories.summary: LLMQuant's Macro Radar Skill calls a live data server to pull CPI, 10-year Treasury yields, Fed Funds, and prices for equities, bonds, and Bitcoin, then returns a structured brief with alignment and divergence reads. Single-source, no independent benchmark. If you run a manual morning macro process, clone the prompt pattern and test it against your next CPI print.

stories.headline: A plugin turns Claude Code's chat output into auditable delivery evidence
stories.summary: Claude Code Harness (MIT-licensed, 1,730 GitHub stars) wraps agent coding in a five-verb loop: spec, plan, work, review, release. Two files act as the source of truth; the agent cannot silently invent data it hasn't seen. Single-source coverage with no independent benchmarks, so clone the repo and run it against a real contract before committing it to your delivery workflow.

sections.intro_body: Today's releases automate real work, but both come with single-source claims and no independent benchmarks. Useful starting points; verify the outputs before you trust the pipeline.

sections.name: currents

stories.headline: Telling a stuck agent why it failed hands attackers an advantage
stories.summary: Redwood Research finds that telling a blocked agent why it failed (the approach used in Claude Code and Codex auto-review) leaks information an adversarial model can exploit. Silent resampling held up better. Single-lab result, no independent replication yet. If this holds, retry-with-feedback is a liability in high-stakes pipelines; if not, the evaluation method needs scrutiny first.

sections.intro_lead: Agent safety has a feedback problem.
sections.intro_body: Early findings suggest that making AI agents more transparent about failure may create new attack surfaces. The evidence is thin, but the direction is worth watching.
